title: Japanese Chahan (Fried Rice)

ingredients.name: cooked rice (preferably day-old, refrigerated)
visual_checks: day-old rice is drier and fries without steaming — fresh rice produces mushy chahan
ingredients.notes: day-old rice is drier and fries without steaming — fresh rice produces mushy chahan

subs.name: crumbled firm tofu stir-fried briefly for a vegan version — season tofu with a pinch of turmeric for colour

ingredients.name: spring onion, finely sliced (white and green parts separated)
mushrooms (shiitake or button), finely diced
visual_checks: adds umami; omit for a simpler, purer chahan
ingredients.notes: adds umami; omit for a simpler, purer chahan

subs.name: frozen peas, finely diced carrot, or corn kernels — add at the same stage and cook until heated through
coconut aminos for a soy-free version in the same quantity

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
visual_checks: added at the end to preserve fragrance
ingredients.notes: added at the end to preserve fragrance

subs.name: a few drops of mustard oil for a pungent Indian note; or omit — the rice is already well-seasoned

steps.text: If the rice has been refrigerated, break up any large clumps with your fingers before cooking. Beat the eggs well with a pinch of salt and set aside. Make sure all other ingredients are prepped and within arm's reach — chahan moves fast once the pan is hot.

Heat a large kadai or wok over maximum flame for 2 minutes until very hot. Add cooking oil and swirl to coat. Pour in the beaten eggs and stir rapidly for 30 to 40 seconds, breaking them into large, soft, barely-set curds — remove from the pan while they are still slightly wet.

Add a little more oil if the pan looks dry. Add the white parts of the spring onion and the mushrooms (if using), and stir-fry for 1 to 2 minutes over high heat until the mushrooms begin to shrink and colour.

Add all the cold rice to the pan. Use the back of a spatula to press and break up any remaining clumps, tossing the rice continuously over high heat for 2 to 3 minutes until each grain is separate, hot and slightly toasted on the outside.

Pour soy sauce around the edge of the pan (not directly on the rice) so it hits the hot metal and caramelises slightly. Toss everything to distribute the sauce evenly, then return the scrambled egg to the pan and fold through the rice.

Turn off the heat. Drizzle sesame oil over the rice and toss once more. Taste and adjust with salt and black pepper. Scatter the green parts of the spring onion and sesame seeds over the top. Serve immediately — chahan loses its texture as it sits.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: egg → crumbled firm tofu stir-fried briefly for a vegan version — season tofu with a pinch of turmeric for colour (changes diet to vegan); soy-sauce → coconut aminos for a soy-free version in the same quantity; gingelly-oil → a few drops of mustard oil for a pungent Indian note; or omit — the rice is already well-seasoned; mushroom → frozen peas, finely diced carrot, or corn kernels — add at the same stage and cook until heated through
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A large pre-heated kadai on highest gas flame is the ideal Indian vessel for chahan — the technique is virtually identical to making egg rice or leftover rice stir-fry, which is already common in Indian homes. Day-old sona masoori or ponni rice works excellently. Soy sauce and sesame oil are the only special-pantry items; both are available at major Indian supermarkets. Add a pinch of white pepper for a restaurant-style touch.
